full_text:
Appeal to the editor in Science Translational Medicine

We thank the reviewers for their overall positive comments. They find our work intriguing and promising, and they appreciate our detailed analyses of difficult-to-get samples.

The main criticisms were mostly on the small sample numbers and correlations to clinical data. Since the submission, we have already generated a significant amount of new data supporting the findings of the manuscript. If allowed for 3 months time to revise, we will be able to add even more data and address all the comments by the reviewers, which in general were in favor of publishing our work.

The main improvements will be:

1) providing the clinical outcome correlations up to 25-30 for patients

2) addition of up to 10 patients for the tCycIF TME and biomarker analysis

3) detailed single-cell analysis of immune activation after the treatments

These additional data will comprehensively addres all the comments from the reviewers and significantly strengthen the conclusions of the study.

Below please find the point-by-point rebuttal letter addressing all the Reviewer’s comments.

Reviewer: 1

This manuscript describes a novel approach to high throughput in vitro drug screening in patient-derived organoids from women with ovarian cancer. The authors describe advantages to this approach including the ability to evaluate treatment effects on both tumor and immune cell populations, and the ability to assess the efficacy of immune therapy combination regimens. They state that prior organoid models were assessed for immune cell function but here the focus was on cytotoxic effects and tumor intrinsic biomarkers of response. Strengths of the manuscript include the attention to potential confounding effects of culture conditions with the observation that tissue dissociation methods could induce a stress response associated with chemoresistance, the analysis of tumor samples collected at secondary debulking to evaluate treatment resistant samples, and the quantification of treatment effects on immune cells as a potential toxicity to consider in establishing dosing. Weaknesses of this report are primarily related to the small sample sizes and the lack of clinical correlation for validation in most cases. Because of this, some of the conclusions are overstated - such as the assertion that this model system can stratify patients into likely responders and nonresponders to treatment.

Because of this, some of the conclusions are overstated - such as the assertion that this model system can stratify patients into likely responders and nonresponders to treatment. This approach requires substantial clinical validation prior to advancing the model as a strategy to assign patients to treatment regimens.

We thank the reviewer for constructive feedback on our manuscript and highlighting the strengths of this study.

Specific issues that should be addressed by the authors prior to publication include:

There is clinical correlation for PARPi treatment efficacy in samples from PDS available for only one patient. The association between HRD and PARPi sensitivity in vitro is well established, but results in patients are more complex, particularly with evidence that PARPi have immunomodulatory effects. It is unclear whether the inclusion of an immune component in the iPDC can improve prediction beyond tumor specific in vitro cultures without a comparison with patient data for validation.

We acknowledge that the numbers were small in the original manuscript. We have now collected clinical data for PARPi and or platinum sensitivity from 20 patients which will be correlated with responses from corresponding iPDCs. Given 3 months time for the revisions, we can provide analyses of up to 25-30 samples significantly strengthening the conclusions of the manuscript.

While it is true that the pro and anti-tumor immunomodulatory effects of PARPi have been established (1) through migration and activation of immune cells and by impairing DNA damage in immune cells and upregulation of PD-L1 on tumor cells. We focused on validating the iPDC model by evaluating the effect of PARPi on cancer cells to clinically validated / predicted HRD-specific anti-tumor response. However, we have now also performed flow cytometry analysis from PARPi treated iPDCs. From this data we can analyze activation states of T cells, Dendritic cells, and NK cells to evaluate immune cell-specific responses to PARPi. We can provide this result during the revision stage along with the clinical correlation to PARPi sensitivity.

While the authors are to be commended for investigating samples from patients with recurrent disease after treatment, they only had samples from 3 people with markedly different treatment histories. While two are described as having progressed on PARPi and chemo, it’s not clear if they would still be considered platinum sensitive based on the diagram in Fig 3A or platinum resistant. Response to PARPi correlates with platinum sensitivity so this would be helpful information to have. Additionally, there is no description of what chemo regimens they received or why they underwent secondary debulking-which is relatively uncommon after 2-3 recurrences, particularly in the setting of disease resistant to multiple chemo regimens. Furthermore, it appears that the investigators do not have data from pre-treatment samples from these same patients to enable them to identify changes associated with chemoresistance – they cite changes in estrogen, TGFb, WNT as being implicated in resistance but don’t have information about baseline expression. Finally, it does not appear that these patients received any of the tested agents with the exception of the patient who continued on olaparib, so there is no clinical correlation available to validate the translational relevance of iPDC outcomes or the associated genomic pathway analyses.

atient who continued on olaparib, so there is no clinical correlation available to validate the translational relevance of iPDC outcomes or the associated genomic pathway analyses. Ultimately, the data in Fig 3 and the analysis of drug sensitivities in resistant tumors present detailed descriptions of three cases but are not sufficient to support the conclusion that “iPDCs enable investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients”.

The three recurrent samples analyzed in this manuscript are representative cases to evaluate if iPDCs can mimic clinically known drug resistance, as well as guide in the identification of effective drugs or combinations in the relapse settings. These three patients underwent secondary debulking surgeries due to oligometastatic disease, and due to no prior debulking surgery. The two patients #C218 and #C429 progressed on PARPi and chemotherapy, respectively they were considered as platinum sensitive base on the primary response to platinum-free interval (PFI) which was 24 months (#C218) and 20 months (#C429). Although they showed initial sensitivity to platinum and PARPi, later developed progressive disease. We will provide a detailed supplementary table for chemotherapy regimens and response table for these three patients.

The pre-treatment samples are available for 2 of the patients - and we have performed WES on the pre-treatment samples, from this data we can analyze mutations and other genetic alterations in ER, TGFb, and WNT signaling to evaluate baseline status of these pathways. If time allows, we can also perform tCycIF single-cell spatial analysis to investigate the tumor-immune landscapes of the pre- and post-treatment samples including ER, TGFb, and WNT signaling. These additional analyses will strengthen our approach of integrating omics analyses with iPDC drug response data to uncover drug sensitivity and resistance in therapy resistant patients.

We acknowledge that the conclusion of “iPDCs enable investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients” is not sufficient based on three samples. Hence, we will tone it down to “iPDCs may aid in the investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients”.

Although the emphasis here is on tumor-intrinsic factors like cytotoxicity, the innovation of the iPDC model is the ability to assess immunologic effects of therapy and the efficacy of immune therapy – tumor extrinsic effects. Because the iPDC can only be maintained for a short time, the analyses of effects on immune cells are restricted to immediate effects on pre-existing tumor infiltrating or tumor-associated lymphocytes. Other mechanisms contributing to the efficacy of immune therapy, such as activation of new T cell populations recognizing neoantigens or changes in T cell recruitment to tumor sites, would be missed in the iPDC system. Induction of immune memory also cannot be assessed.

As the reviewer pointed out, the limitation of iPDCs is that it only allows investigation of immunotherapy effects on intra-tumoral immune cells but not on the mobility and priming of the peripheral blood immune cells. Despite this limitation, the fact that iPDCs harbor patient-matched intratumor immune cells makes them a useful preclinical model to evaluate effectiveness of immuno-oncology agents in activating intra-tumoral immune cells. We will include this point in the Discussion.

Some of the immunomodulatory effects attributed to the anti-tumor agents tested may instead result from co-culture with dying tumor cells. Increased tumor cell death with release of DAMPs would be expected to enhance DC activation and subsequent T cell activation. No controls were evaluated to distinguish direct effects on leukocyte subsets from indirect effects in coculture with tumor.

We agree that dying tumor cells might cause some of the immunomodulatory effects in iPDCs and in patient tumors. Therefore, we believe that assessing the immunomodulatory effects on monocultures of leukocyte subsets may not be translationally relevant, as it does not mimic tumor and immune cell interactions observed in the native tumors. However, we always normalize the results to un-treated control to exclude culture-induced changes in tumor and immune cell functional states. This will be clarified further in the manuscript.

In addition, leukocyte attrition resulting from time in culture was not explicitly considered. Although the authors assert that no significant changes in leukocyte distribution was observed in different iPDC preparation conditions or over time, there are marked differences in the distribution of leukocyte subsets in the iPDC compared with parental tumors in Figure 2B suggesting that engraftment in culture may have edited or enriched leukocyte populations.

While we observed heterogeneity in the immune cell proportions, these did not reach statistical significance. Moreover, it is expected that any ex vivo model is subjected to some biological change compared to the native tissue due to tissue processing and in vitro culture conditions. We will include this in the Discussion to acknowledge the changes in the abundance of immune cells present in iPDCs compared to source tumor.

Comparing death of tumor cells with death of immune cells based on proportions of each is an unusual assessment of immune toxicity of treatment. Lymphocyte toxicity may reflect the proliferative status of subsets which could vary by patient and by treatment. It’s difficult to draw conclusions about these complex interactions with a simple ratio. Additionally, in patients, leukopenia is attributed to marrow suppression, which cannot be captured here.

The experiments are performed in a short time frame - whereby the effects of only direct toxicities of the drugs to the different cell populations (tumor-immune cells) in the iPDCs. In the patients, bone-marrow toxicity where the peripheral blood leukocytes are typically at the lowest point at 10 days after chemotherapy, which is beyond the experiments in the iPDCs. Therefore - our approach is focused on the tumor-specific effects of the treatments. This limitation will be further clarified in the Manuscript and Discussion.

The biomarker analysis is based on responders among a cohort of 8 patients. Assessing expression of a candidate biomarker on a cellular level may support further investigation but is not sufficient to draw conclusions about biomarkers to assign patients to treatment.

During the revisions, we will be able to add up to 10 more iPDC and patients to this analysis to strengthen the data and conclusions.

The TOPACIO trial is cited as a justification for investigations of HRD tumors. It is important to note that the trial tested combined PARPi and PD1 ICI, not PD1 ICI monotherapy, and no correlation between tumor HRD status and response was observed in patients. These data and other clinical trial results showing limited efficacy of PD1 ICI are difficult to reconcile with the comparatively high response to PD1 ICI in iPDC. The authors suggest that this may be because patients receive PD1 after prior chemo, but frontline ICI combination trials in OC have also shown limited benefit. This discrepancy highlights the need for additional translational validation of this model system prior to implementation.

In our work by Färkkilä et al., describing the translational work on the TOPACIO clincial trial, we demonstrated a significant correlation of HRD and PFS to the combination of PARPi + PD-1 ICI (2).

Frontline ICI combination trials in ovarian cancer are still waiting for results: currently, four large Phase III clinical trials are ongoing (ENGOT-OV43/KEYLYNK-001, DUO-O, ATHENA-COMBO, ENGOT-OV44/ FIRST) investigating the efficacy of 1.st line IO + chemo combinations, with the first results expected to be released in Q2/2025. Therefore, it is currently unclear to which patients ICI should be administered in ovarian cancer, highlighting the timeliness of our efforts in identification of patients responding to ICI.

As the frontline ICI efficacies in HGSC are unknown, our model highlights the genotype (HRD) specific responses in the patients. For additional translational validation, we will further perform tCycIF analysis on the iPDC tumors to verify how well the iPDCs correspond to the patient tumors.

Reviewer: 2

This manuscript by Nagaraj and colleagues tested a novel PDX and organoid model to study clinical predictive effects of the model in ovarian cancer. The model is interesting, and the data are generally easy to follow, but some data as noted below are hard to understand. The applicability to broad patient populations is hard to support from these data. This work, while intriguing and promising seems better suited for a specialty journal at this stage.

The model applicability to broad patient populations. For example, tissue-specific matrices have been shown to improve predictions in lymphoid metastases (3).

Omentum is the most frequent site of intr-abodminal metastases in cancer (4)). Therefore, the OmGel applications will be highly relevant for a broad range of common cancers frequently metastasizing to the omentum - e.g. endometrial, cervical, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic cancers.

Details

Fig. 1 Data that on differential effects of enzymes and BME versus OmGel are good. Data that cells in the OmGel preparations are olaparib-sensitive are good, but there are too few samples to conclude that this assay predicts patient response reliably.

The conclusions of the predictions will be strengthened by the addition of up to 25-30 iPDC cultures and the correlations to clinical data.

Fig. 2 Data that immune cells are represented in various in vitro preparations are good, but the subset analyses are difficult to understand. For example, the IFNγ, GzB and Ki67 data in 2F are not really comprehensible. How were the differences in expression determined? What is the flow gating strategy?

The data in Figure 2F shows the Log2FC ín the mean intensity of functional markers in CD8+ T cells from HRD or HRP iPDCs upon Olaparib or Pembrolizumab treatment. The fold change in the mean intensity of each marker is normalized to untreated control. We will clarify this detail in the corresponding Result section of the manuscript. The flow cytometry analysis for immune cell abundance was performed using CYTO (5) from the EpCAM- cell populations as shown in Supplementary Figure 2A. We will update this Figure to also include the functional marker analysis per immune cell type.

Fig. 3 Response data are interesting but insufficient to make claims of predicting patient responses in general populations.

The conclusions of the predictions will be strengthened by the providing up to 25-30 iPDC cultures and the correlations to clinical response data for platinum and PARPi treatments.

Fig. 4 The capacity for high throughput screens are interesting but hard to understand how predictive the data will be for general patient populations. This reviewer cannot find any reported information on OmGel.

The conclusions of the predictions will be strengthened by the providing of up to 25-30 iPDC iPDC cultures and the correlations to clinical data. Full proteomic analysis and the relevance of Omentum Gel has been published by Neilson et al (6), and this it has been cited in the manuscript “Introduction line 99” and in the “Discussion line 311”.

Minor

The writing and grammar are excellent, but could nonetheless benefit from a native English speaker.

We will have the manuscript proofread by a native English speaker.

REFERENCES
